role_hints.oews_basis: 53-7061 Cleaners of Vehicles and Equipment 39.0% of wage bill
role_hints.note: Physical hand labor concentrated in full-service, flex-serve and detailing formats. The historical labor replacement here — express exterior tunnels replacing wash crews with machinery — is decades-old capital equipment, not AI; do not credit AI with tunnel automation.

role_hints.oews_basis: 53-6031 Automotive and Watercraft Service Attendants 10.7% + 41-2011 Cashiers 3.0%
role_hints.note: Loading/guiding, prep, membership selling at the pay station — license-plate recognition, kiosk/app payment and automated gates (adjacent automation, some AI content) are already shrinking this role at express sites.

role_hints.oews_basis: 49-3023 Automotive Service Technicians 9.0% + 49-1011 Supervisors of Mechanics 2.3% + 49-3093 Tire Repairers 1.5% — NOTE: role_mix is 5-digit 81119, which includes 811191 oil-change/lube shops; the auto-tech and tire occupations are largely sibling-code contamination
role_hints.approx_share_of_wage_bill: ~13% in the proxy; true car-wash-only share is lower (tunnel equipment techs)
role_hints.note: Physical equipment upkeep; strip the lube-shop contamination when modeling car-wash labor.

role_hints.oews_basis: 53-1047 First-Line Supervisors of Transportation/Material Moving Workers 9.5% + 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 10.1%
role_hints.note: Multi-site chains centralize scheduling, pricing, membership analytics and labor planning — AI compresses the above-site layer; on-site supervision persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 43-4051 Customer Service Representatives 1.2% + 43-9061 Office Clerks 1.3% + admin tail
role_hints.note: Membership billing support, chargeback handling, bookkeeping — small base, cleanly automatable.

source_hints.source: International Carwash Association (ICA) industry research and consumer studies
source_hints.why: The trade association's data on format mix, wash counts and technology trends.

source_hints.source: Amplify Car Wash Advisors market commentary and deal announcements
source_hints.why: Specialist car-wash M&A advisor; publishes commentary on multiples, buyer appetite and the post-2022 cool-down.

source_hints.source: Car Wash Advisory (specialist investment bank) market reports
source_hints.why: Publishes car wash M&A and valuation reports covering single-site vs chain multiples.

source_hints.source: Mister Car Wash (NYSE: MCW) and Driven Brands (Take 5 Car Wash) public filings
source_hints.why: Real unit economics: membership (UWC) penetration, ARPU, churn, site-level labor and margins — the benchmark for what a mature express operator looks like.

source_hints.source: Professional Carwashing & Detailing magazine industry surveys
source_hints.why: Operator-level surveys reaching the independent/legacy-format owners who make up the fragmented tail.

capture_questions: Pricing is direct-to-consumer retail: single washes and unlimited monthly memberships, with no payer, fee schedule or auditor above the operator — so any cost saving is retained margin unless competition takes it. The real pricing question is tunnel oversupply: with heavy new-build through 2019-2023, are trade areas seeing membership price wars and discounting that competes away site-level gains, and what do MCW/Driven membership ARPU and churn disclosures show?

Membership economics are the moat claim: what share of revenue is subscription (unlimited plans), what are churn and ARPU trends, and does the subscription habit plus location convenience actually hold customers when a competitor tunnel opens across the street?

What does the acquirer of a band-size operator ($1-10M EBITDA, roughly the 100-499 and upper 500+ classes — n_band 186) actually buy: express tunnels with membership books, or legacy full-serve/flex sites whose labor-heavy model is being competed out by express formats? The two have opposite trajectories and different multiples.

How much of the value sits in real estate rather than operations — sale-leaseback prevalence, cap-rate-driven site values, rent burdens left behind after PE sale-leasebacks — and is the operating company worth much once market rent is charged?
terminal_value_question: A car wash is a machine plus a site: AI does not wash cars, and the big labor-removal event (express tunnels replacing wash crews) is mature machinery capex that already happened — so the honest AI question is whether there is any material AI-labor channel at all beyond site-level admin, membership analytics and above-site management (perhaps 15-25% of an already modest wage bill), or whether this is a consumer-subscription/real-estate business whose roll-up math must stand entirely without AI cost extraction. Demand survives trivially; the failure mode to guard against is back-filling an AI thesis onto a non-AI consolidation — if the AI-attributable EBITDA uplift rounds to a small number, the score should say so.
special_notes: Sibling contamination: role_mix is 5-digit 81119, which folds in 811191 oil-change/lube shops — automotive service technicians and tire repairers in the mix are largely not car-wash staff; strip them before setting the V structure.

Format heterogeneity drives everything: express exterior (low labor ~10-15 FTE/site, membership-led), full-service/flex (labor-heavy, declining share), self-serve bays (coin-op, near-zero labor), and detailing — labor share, automation exposure and multiples differ by format; identify which formats populate the $1-10M EBITDA band before scoring.

Real estate dominates the capital story: sale-leasebacks are pervasive — EBITDA vs EBITDAR (rent-adjusted) comparisons are decisive; a site 'multiple' often embeds land value and a below/above-market lease. Owner-operated legacy sites also carry owner comp and historical cash revenue in their P&Ls — normalize both.

Membership accounting: unlimited-wash plans create deferred revenue and churn sensitivity; 'revenue growth' can be member-count growth with falling ARPU — check cohort disclosures where available (MCW filings).

The consolidation wave (Mister, Take 5/Driven, Mammoth, GO, ZIPS, Express Wash Concepts, PE platforms) peaked around 2021-22 and cooled sharply with interest rates and oversupply — verify current deal pace and multiples rather than assuming peak-era figures; B/M use only (rule 2).

n_band note: unusually, the 500+ class (62 firms, avg revenue $60M) lands IN the $1-10M EBITDA band at the assumed 15.65% margin — the band here includes sizable regional chains, not just the 100-499 class; express-format EBITDA margins (25-35% site-level) are well above the generic margin used, so band membership is margin-sensitive in both directions.
